title: Scrotal Redness and Swelling in Cats: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Identify causes of scrotal swelling in cats. Learn why this condition requires prompt care and how photos help your vet triage the issue.
content_html:
TL;DR: Scrotal redness and swelling in cats is a painful condition that requires veterinary evaluation within 24 hours to treat potential infection and manage your pet's discomfort.

What is scrotal redness and swelling in cats?

Scrotal redness and swelling, also known as scrotal dermatitis or orchitis, is a painful inflammatory response in the skin surrounding the testicles or the testicles themselves. Common indicators and causes include:

  • Bright red skin that is warm to the touch.
  • Significant swelling where the area appears larger than normal.
  • Local trauma, bacterial infections, insect bites, or contact allergies to bedding and cleaning supplies.

Is scrotal swelling and redness in my cat an emergency?

The urgency for this condition is medium; while rarely immediately life-threatening, it causes intense discomfort. You should seek veterinary care within 24 hours, especially if you notice:

  • Lethargy or a reduced appetite.
  • Excessive licking, biting, or self-mutilation of the area.
  • Signs of potential systemic infection.

How does providing a photo of my cat's symptoms help with triage?

Sharing a clear, well-lit photo of the affected area allows a veterinary professional to prioritize your pet's needs by:

  • Differentiating between localized skin irritation and serious conditions like a scrotal abscess or hernia.
  • Assessing the severity of inflammation to determine urgency.
  • Advising on home management steps to keep your cat comfortable until the appointment.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, scrotal redness and swelling can be attributed to orchitis and epididymitis, although these are rare unless caused by trauma. Potential causes include trauma, infection (fungal, bacterial, or viral), or testicular torsion. Clinical signs include pain and swelling of the testes, epididymides, and/or scrotum, and there may be wounds or other lesions on the scrotal skin. Palpation of the scrotal contents is important, but ultrasonography, with sedation or analgesia as needed, can further evaluate the affected structures and confirm the presence of testicular torsion or focal lesions such as masses or abscesses. Ultrasonography can also identify less common causes of scrotal enlargement such as scrotal hernia or hematoma.

Chapter: Urology, Infectious Disease, Surgery

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1400)

faqs.question: Can I use over-the-counter creams on my cat?
faqs.answer: No, you should avoid applying any human creams or ointments without veterinary approval, as cats often lick these areas and many human medications are toxic if ingested.

faqs.question: What can I do to keep my cat comfortable?
faqs.answer: The most important step is to prevent your cat from licking the area. Using an Elizabethan collar (a cone) will stop further irritation and self-trauma until you can see a vet.

faqs.question: What are the common symptoms to watch for?
faqs.answer: Look for visible redness, heat in the area, reluctance to move, a stiff-legged gait, or vocalizing when the hindquarters are touched.
